news_summary: Today’s human rights progress includes the UN advancing road safety commitments despite opposition, Indigenous nations asserting greater influence amid climate threats, and France pioneering protections for children online with a social media ban for under-15s.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées enregistrées aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme, on peut citer les efforts déployés par l’ONU pour faire progresser les engagements en faveur de la sécurité routière malgré l’opposition, l’influence croissante des nations autochtones face aux menaces climatiques, ainsi que la France, qui fait figure de pionnière en matière de protection des enfants en ligne grâce à l’interdiction d’accès aux réseaux sociaux pour les moins de 15 ans.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos destacan el impulso de la ONU a los compromisos en materia de seguridad vial a pesar de la oposición, la mayor influencia que están ejerciendo las naciones indígenas ante las amenazas climáticas y la iniciativa pionera de Francia para proteger a los menores en Internet mediante la prohibición del uso de las redes sociales a los menores de 15 años.

articles.title: ‘Business as usual is a business of death’: UN adopts road safety pledge over US objection
articles.summary: The UN General Assembly closed its first ever High-level Meeting on road safety on Tuesday by adopting a sweeping new declaration aimed at halving road deaths by 2030 – over the sole objection of the United States.
articles.content:
The Progress Declaration passed by a recorded vote of 162 in favour to one against, with no abstentions.

It commits governments to strengthen national road safety systems, upgrade vehicle and infrastructure standards, and adopt the ‘Safe System’ approach, which designs roads and vehicles to survive the mistakes drivers inevitably make.

Washington cries mission creep

The US representative accused the declaration of “mandate creep that has eroded the credibility of this institution,” objecting to provisions linking road safety with climate and environmental policy.

Tweet URL

“Road safety is a practical matter of engineering, sober driving and personal responsibility,” he said. It “should not be used as a backdoor vehicle to enforce global climate pacts and top-down environmental restrictions on freedom of mobility.”

Other countries backed the declaration while marking their own reservations.

Argentina stressed its sovereign right to implement it according to national law, while separately reserving its position on the World Health Organization’s (WHO) role. Paraguay underlined that the follow-up process, like the declaration itself, is voluntary and non-binding.

‘More than the number killed in direct conflict’

Opening the meeting, General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock addressed the question she said many people ask: why does the UN spend its time on road safety when wars and crises dominate the agenda?

The answer, she said, is scale. Road crashes kill more than 1.2 million people a year and injure some 50 million more – more, she noted, than are killed in direct conflict, murdered, or die of malaria.

“Since I began speaking,” she told delegates, several more families had already received news that would change their lives forever.

She asked delegates to think of the everyday journeys that bring people to work, school or home – “whether you came by car, by bike or simply walked” to UN Headquarters, all had taken part in “the complex choreography of New York’s traffic system.”

The issue is personal for her too: her aunt was killed by a tram, a loss whose legacy, she said, “lingered”, sending “never-ending ripples of devastation” through the family.

Progress, but nowhere near fast enough

New WHO data released alongside the declaration show the global road death rate fell 21 per cent between 2011 and 2025, even as more than a billion vehicles were added to the world’s roads.

Still, 1.16 million people died in crashes in 2025, and road injuries remain the leading killer of children and young people aged 5 to 29.

WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus opened his remarks by asking delegates who had been in a crash or had lost or known someone killed or injured in one, to stand. “I think almost all of us,” he said. “All road deaths are preventable and none are acceptable.”

The gains are wildly uneven. Deaths fell 36 per cent in Europe and 15 per cent in the Western Pacific, but just two per cent in South-East Asia; they were flat in the Americas and up 17 per cent in Africa.

Motorcyclists, whose numbers have more than tripled since 2011, now account for nearly a third of all road deaths – though WHO says a certified helmet cuts the risk of dying more than sixfold.

As the UN’s Special Envoy for Road Safety, Jean Todt, put it: “Business as usual is a business of death.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The UN General Assembly adopted a significant global declaration aimed at halving road deaths by 2030, committing governments worldwide to improve road safety systems, vehicle and infrastructure standards, and adopt safer road design approaches. This represents a major international policy effort with broad public health and safety implications, addressing a leading cause of death globally. The article provides detailed context on the scale of the problem, the commitments made, and the challenges ahead, making it a substantive and impactful story with hopeful prospects for saving millions of lives.
articles.personality_title: UN commits to halving road deaths by 2030 with new global safety pledge
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, over 1 million people die in road crashes worldwide, and many more are injured. Road accidents kill more people than wars or diseases like malaria, making road safety a critical global issue.

**What happened** – The United Nations General Assembly adopted a new declaration called the Progress Declaration during its first High-level Meeting on road safety. The declaration, agreed on June 2024 by 162 countries, aims to cut road deaths by half by 2030. It asks governments to improve road safety systems, vehicle and road standards, and use the 'Safe System' approach, which designs roads to protect everyone, even if drivers make mistakes. The United States was the only country to vote against it.

**Impact** – This declaration is important because it sets a clear and strong international goal to save millions of lives. It acknowledges that road safety is not just about individual behavior but also about better roads, safer cars, and smarter planning. The commitment shows a worldwide effort to reduce injuries and deaths, especially among children and young people, who are most at risk. It also highlights the need to protect motorcyclists, whose numbers and risks have grown.

**What's next step** – Countries will now work on putting the declaration into action by improving laws, building safer roads, and raising awareness about road safety. This effort will be monitored over the coming years to track progress. The World Health Organization and the UN will support countries, but the declaration itself is voluntary, meaning each country decides how to apply it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new global pledge aims to cut road deaths by half by 2030 through safer roads, vehicles, and policies, marking a major step toward protecting millions of lives worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU s’engage à réduire de moitié les décès routiers d’ici 2030 avec une nouvelle déclaration mondiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, plus d’un million de personnes meurent dans des accidents de la route dans le monde, et beaucoup d’autres sont blessées. Les accidents de la route tuent plus de personnes que les guerres ou des maladies comme le paludisme, ce qui fait de la sécurité routière un enjeu mondial crucial.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Assemblée générale des Nations Unies a adopté une nouvelle déclaration appelée la Déclaration de progrès lors de sa première réunion de haut niveau sur la sécurité routière. Cette déclaration, adoptée en juin 2024 par 162 pays, vise à réduire de moitié les décès routiers d’ici 2030. Elle demande aux gouvernements d’améliorer les systèmes de sécurité routière, les normes des véhicules et des infrastructures, et d’adopter l’approche « Système sûr », qui conçoit les routes pour protéger tout le monde, même si les conducteurs font des erreurs. Les États-Unis ont été le seul pays à voter contre.

**Impact** – Cette déclaration est importante car elle fixe un objectif international clair pour sauver des millions de vies. Elle reconnaît que la sécurité routière ne dépend pas seulement du comportement individuel, mais aussi de meilleures routes, de voitures plus sûres et d’une planification intelligente. Cet engagement montre un effort mondial pour réduire les blessures et les décès, surtout chez les enfants et les jeunes, qui sont les plus exposés. Il souligne aussi la nécessité de protéger les motocyclistes, dont le nombre et les risques ont augmenté.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays vont maintenant travailler à la mise en œuvre de la déclaration en améliorant les lois, en construisant des routes plus sûres et en sensibilisant à la sécurité routière. Ces efforts seront suivis dans les années à venir pour mesurer les progrès. L’Organisation mondiale de la santé et l’ONU aideront les pays, mais la déclaration reste volontaire, chaque pays décidant comment l’appliquer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle déclaration mondiale de l’ONU vise à réduire de moitié les décès routiers d’ici 2030 grâce à des routes, des véhicules et des politiques plus sûrs, marquant une avancée majeure pour protéger des millions de vies.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU se compromete a reducir a la mitad las muertes en carretera para 2030 con nueva declaración global
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, más de un millón de personas mueren en accidentes de tráfico en todo el mundo, y muchas más resultan heridas. Los accidentes de carretera causan más muertes que guerras o enfermedades como la malaria, por lo que la seguridad vial es un problema global muy importante.

**Qué pasó** – La Asamblea General de las Naciones Unidas adoptó una nueva declaración llamada Declaración de Progreso durante su primera Reunión de Alto Nivel sobre seguridad vial. La declaración, aprobada en junio de 2024 por 162 países, tiene como objetivo reducir a la mitad las muertes en carretera para 2030. Pide a los gobiernos mejorar los sistemas de seguridad vial, las normas de vehículos e infraestructuras, y aplicar el enfoque del 'Sistema Seguro', que diseña las carreteras para proteger a todos, incluso si los conductores cometen errores. Estados Unidos fue el único país que votó en contra.

**Impacto** – Esta declaración es importante porque establece una meta internacional clara para salvar millones de vidas. Reconoce que la seguridad vial no depende solo del comportamiento individual, sino también de mejores carreteras, vehículos más seguros y una planificación inteligente. El compromiso muestra un esfuerzo mundial para reducir lesiones y muertes, especialmente entre niños y jóvenes, que son los más afectados. También destaca la necesidad de proteger a los motociclistas, cuyo número y riesgos han aumentado.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora los países trabajarán para poner en práctica la declaración mejorando leyes, construyendo carreteras más seguras y aumentando la conciencia sobre seguridad vial. Este esfuerzo será monitoreado en los próximos años para seguir el progreso. La Organización Mundial de la Salud y la ONU apoyarán a los países, pero la declaración es voluntaria, y cada país decidirá cómo aplicarla.

**Frase clave** – La nueva declaración global de la ONU busca reducir a la mitad las muertes en carretera para 2030 mediante carreteras, vehículos y políticas más seguros, marcando un paso importante para proteger millones de vidas en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a gently winding road shaped like a protective hand cradling diverse symbolic vehicles—bicycles, motorcycles with helmets, and cars—traveling safely through a calm landscape under a soft sky, illustrating careful design and shared responsibility in road safety without any human figures.

articles.title: As climate change threatens their lands, Indigenous nations demand more power at the UN
articles.content:
This story is published through the Indigenous News Alliance.

The Arctic is warming four times faster than other regions, a trend that disproportionately harms the Indigenous Sámi people — particularly their access to vital traditional resources like reindeer and salmon, which are used for food, clothing, weapons, and more.

As northern temperatures rise, snow is melting and refreezing at an accelerating rate. That forms ice, preventing reindeer from reaching the lichen below and dramatically increasing starvation rates. “Those impacts in nature are severe for our culture and livelihoods,” said Tuomas Aslak Juuso, vice president of the Sámediggi, or Sámi Parliament, of Finland. “So survival of reindeer and then survival of our livelihoods [and] cultures is then questioned.”

Last week, the impacts of climate change and colonialism were among the key issues raised by the hundreds of Indigenous people gathered in Geneva. They’d come from around the world to discuss these mounting threats at the United Nations Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, or EMRIP. They agreed that a multitude of crises will only grow worse unless they are given greater say in international negotiations and decision-making. That’s why in addition to pushing specific policy recommendations on conflict, AI, and other key issues, Indigenous delegates continued their decades-long demand for better representation at the U.N.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“What we are trying to do is to get some implementation resources for us to adapt to the climate change. With Finland, for example, we don’t have any adaptation resources on how to adapt to these situations,” Juuso said. “To be able to bring these issues forward to the global discussion, we would have to have the opportunity to participate.”

Member states are granted access to U.N. gatherings, where they can easily express views, determine policies, and outline their progress implementing international commitments required by the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples and other agreements. But Indigenous advocates say the voices of the peoples directly impacted by such actions aren’t always heard at international forums.

The U.N. structure requires participation through either a member state or a registered civil society agency — for instance, a non-governmental organization, academic institution, or accredited Indigenous organization. But Indigenous nations don’t fit neatly into one of those categories and are reduced to participating as NGOs. Beyond those structural barriers, Indigenous peoples also face financial and other resource challenges that compound the difficulty of making themselves heard at the international level. That’s why those attending EMRIP continued the long fight for what is called enhanced participation, which could finally give Indigenous nations and organizations the same access and power the United States and other countries enjoy.

That increased influence is especially important when many countries ignore Indigenous priorities. Throughout the week, many delegates identified climate and environmental issues as one area where national governments often have different priorities than Indigenous peoples, who consider land and ecosystems more important than economic growth — a value system they believe is at odds with the world’s best interests.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“We’ve got to try and balance immediate economic interests with longer-term environmental protections,” said Kym Hamilton, who is from the tribes of Ngāti Kahungunu, Ngā Rauru, Ngāti Raukawa in Aotearoa New Zealand. “I think we’ve got a responsibility to protect our descendants.”

In U.N. lexicon, enhanced participation is the catchphrase often used for efforts to elevate Indigenous peoples’ voices. Those gathered in Geneva asserted the right to represent their communities in crucial global discussions that impact them. They are also demanding more respect from member-state governments and other policymakers, something they argue will better help them to survive conflict and other crises.

“The question before us is no longer whether Indigenous peoples should participate more fully in the Human Rights Council,” Hamilton, who spoke on behalf of the National Iwi Chairs Forum that represents 87 tribes, said. “The question now is how quickly we will implement these commitments.”

She offered recommendations for EMRIP’s seven expert Indigenous members, including a clear timeline for creating participatory status for Indigenous peoples’ institutions and appointing Indigenous and state representatives with equal standing to lead the process. She also called for the establishment of an independent and transparent accreditation mechanism to prevent states from vetoing Indigenous representatives, and initiatives to provide predictable and sustainable funding to take part.

Hamilton said Indigenous peoples’ full participation in international deliberations includes the right to decide who represents their communities. “Governments don’t always tell the truth,” she said, citing the U.N. Human Rights Council’s Universal Periodic Review — which all 193 member states must undergo every 4.5 years — which she said revealed gaps between the reports governments submitted and the actual human rights situations within their jurisdictions.

Kenneth Deer, member of the Mohawk Nation of Kahnawà:ke and longtime international Indigenous advocate, echoed that point. “The government never speaks ill about itself,” he said. “You can’t let government speak for us.”

But the U.N.’s accreditation and recognition process can hinder progress toward collectively tackling the challenges impacting Indigenous peoples, he noted. “For us, as a representative institution, there isn’t actually any clear path to accreditate ourselves — and that leads to them trying to improvise, and try to find ways how we can accreditate ourselves.”

The National Congress of American Indians also called for increased Indigenous participation at the international level. “Recognition and participation of Indigenous peoples must accommodate the numerous ways we identify, organize, and represent ourselves,” President Mark Macarro said. “It is essential that we participate directly in matters of global concern that affect our environment, health, human rights, and unique rights as Indigenous peoples.”

Deer said he’s seen many caucuses of Indigenous delegates at the U.N. engage in important collaborations. But he wishes they could have a higher profile. “Indigenous peoples want to have a voice,” he said. “They want to be heard.”

A diversity of voices at the U.N.’s highest levels, he believes, would improve the U.N. processes and system to the benefit of all countries. “What we need is the states, now, to agree to a process that was run by Indigenous people,” he said.

Throughout the week, EMRIP delegates identified a host of other barriers to participation. For example, communications technology within remote communities presents challenges to joining global deliberations. Additionally, there are significant financial barriers for under-resourced communities when U.N. forums are held far from their homelands.

The U.N. Voluntary Fund for Indigenous Peoples, which helps finance travel to U.N. forums, is also experiencing budget shortfalls, inhibiting many delegates’ ability to attend. It is seen as an important way to increase participation among delegates from areas where governments do not recognize the Indigenous peoples within their borders. Sheryl Lightfoot, EMRIP’s North America region member, is an Anishinaabe expert in Indigenous rights, global politics, and public policy.

She said states are not involved in how EMRIP officially recognizes Indigenous peoples. “Worldwide, there’s a need — and this is especially true across Africa and Asia — of Indigenous peoples seeking just mere recognition of their existence from their governments,” she said. “States seem to be more resistant now than they were 10 years ago to recognize that peoples are Indigenous peoples and have those rights attached to them.”

Despite Hamilton’s doubts about how much change has been achieved internationally, hearing her peers from other Indigenous nations testify about how they’ve persevered inspired her and restored her hope. “I feel like there’s a real sense of … urgency and concern,” she said. “We don’t want our grandchildren’s grandchildren to have to keep coming here and doing this stuff.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Indigenous nations' ongoing efforts to gain greater representation and influence at the United Nations to address climate change and protect their cultures and livelihoods. It reports a meaningful, real-world advocacy movement with broad significance for Indigenous peoples globally, emphasizing their rights, environmental stewardship, and participation in international decision-making. The story provides detailed context about the challenges faced and the concrete demands for enhanced participation, making it a substantive and inspiring account of Indigenous resilience and activism.
articles.personality_title: Indigenous Nations Push for Greater Voice at the UN to Protect Their Lands from Climate Change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Arctic is warming much faster than other places, which is harming the Sámi people who live there. Their traditional ways, like herding reindeer and fishing for salmon, are at risk because of changing weather and ice conditions.

**What happened** – Recently, hundreds of Indigenous leaders met in Geneva at the United Nations Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (EMRIP). They discussed how climate change and colonial history threaten their lands and cultures. They asked for more power and a stronger role in UN decisions, since current rules limit their participation.

**Impact** – Indigenous peoples want to have the same influence as countries in the UN so their voices are truly heard. This is important because governments often focus on economic growth, while Indigenous communities prioritize protecting the environment and their way of life. Giving Indigenous nations more say could lead to better policies that respect their needs and help preserve their cultures.

**What's next step** – The Indigenous delegates called for clear plans to give their organizations official status at the UN, fair ways to choose their representatives, and steady funding to attend meetings. They want faster action to include them fully in global talks about human rights and the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Indigenous nations are demanding equal participation at the UN to better protect their lands, cultures, and futures from climate change threats.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les nations autochtones réclament une plus grande voix à l’ONU pour protéger leurs terres du changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Arctique se réchauffe beaucoup plus vite que d’autres régions, ce qui nuit aux Sámi, un peuple autochtone. Leur mode de vie traditionnel, comme l’élevage de rennes et la pêche au saumon, est menacé par les changements climatiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, des centaines de dirigeants autochtones se sont réunis à Genève lors du Mécanisme d’experts des Nations unies sur les droits des peuples autochtones (EMRIP). Ils ont parlé des menaces liées au climat et au colonialisme, et ont demandé plus de pouvoir dans les décisions de l’ONU, car les règles actuelles limitent leur participation.

**Impact** – Les peuples autochtones veulent avoir autant d’influence que les pays à l’ONU pour que leurs voix soient réellement entendues. C’est important parce que les gouvernements privilégient souvent la croissance économique, alors que les communautés autochtones veulent protéger l’environnement et leur mode de vie. Plus de pouvoir pourrait conduire à de meilleures politiques qui respectent leurs besoins.

**Prochaine étape** – Les délégués autochtones demandent des plans clairs pour reconnaître officiellement leurs organisations à l’ONU, des façons équitables de choisir leurs représentants, et un financement stable pour participer aux réunions. Ils veulent une action rapide pour les inclure pleinement dans les discussions mondiales sur les droits humains et l’environnement.

**Phrase-clé** – Les nations autochtones réclament une participation égale à l’ONU pour mieux protéger leurs terres, leurs cultures et leur avenir face aux menaces du changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Naciones indígenas exigen mayor voz en la ONU para proteger sus tierras del cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Ártico se está calentando mucho más rápido que otras regiones, lo que afecta a los Sámi, un pueblo indígena. Sus formas tradicionales de vida, como la cría de renos y la pesca de salmón, están en peligro por los cambios climáticos.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, cientos de líderes indígenas se reunieron en Ginebra en el Mecanismo de Expertos de la ONU sobre los Derechos de los Pueblos Indígenas (EMRIP). Hablaron sobre las amenazas del cambio climático y el colonialismo, y pidieron más poder en las decisiones de la ONU, ya que las reglas actuales limitan su participación.

**Impacto** – Los pueblos indígenas quieren tener la misma influencia que los países en la ONU para que sus voces sean realmente escuchadas. Esto es importante porque los gobiernos a menudo se enfocan en el crecimiento económico, mientras que las comunidades indígenas priorizan proteger el medio ambiente y su forma de vida. Tener más poder podría llevar a mejores políticas que respeten sus necesidades.

**Próximo paso** – Los delegados indígenas pidieron planes claros para dar a sus organizaciones estatus oficial en la ONU, formas justas de elegir a sus representantes y financiamiento constante para asistir a las reuniones. Quieren que se actúe rápido para incluirlos completamente en las discusiones globales sobre derechos humanos y medio ambiente.

**Frase clave** – Las naciones indígenas exigen participación igualitaria en la ONU para proteger mejor sus tierras, culturas y futuro frente a las amenazas del cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a resilient reindeer standing on fragile Arctic ice beneath a softly glowing northern sky, with symbolic salmon swimming beneath the cracked ice, all framed by abstract silhouettes of diverse Indigenous hands raised together in unity, rendered in natural, muted earth tones and icy blues.

articles.title: 'France wants to lead the way' with under-15s social media ban
articles.summary: French lawmakers are expected to pass a bill on July 21st banning social media access to children under 15. If approved, France would follow Australia's lead, where the world's first ban ​for under-16s ‌on platforms including Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube came into ⁠force in December, as concerns grow over the impact social media has on the health and safety of minors.
articles.content:
French lawmakers are expected to pass a bill on July 21st banning social media access to children under 15. If approved, France would follow Australia's lead, where the world's first ban ​for under-16s ‌on platforms including Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube came into ⁠force in December, as concerns grow over the impact social media has on the health and safety of minors.

Video by: Clovis CASALI
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant legislative effort to protect children under 15 from potential harms of social media, addressing health and safety concerns for minors. This policy has broad societal impact and represents meaningful progress in child protection and digital rights.
articles.personality_title: France plans to ban social media for children under 15
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries are worried about how social media affects young children’s health and safety. Australia became the first country to ban social media use for kids under 16 in December. Now, France is considering a similar law.

**What happened** – On July 21st, French lawmakers are expected to vote on a new law that would stop children under 15 from using social media platforms like Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok, and YouTube. This law aims to protect young children from harmful content and risks online.

**Impact** – If the law passes, France will join Australia in setting a legal age limit for social media use. This is important because it shows a growing effort to keep children safer online and to address problems like cyberbullying, addiction, and exposure to inappropriate content.

**What's next step** – After the vote, if the law is approved, social media companies will need to enforce age checks to block users under 15 in France. Other countries might watch closely and consider similar rules to protect children.

**One-sentence takeaway** – France is moving forward with a law to ban social media use by children under 15 to improve their safety and well-being online.
articles.personality_title_fr: La France veut interdire les réseaux sociaux aux moins de 15 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plusieurs pays s'inquiètent des effets des réseaux sociaux sur la santé et la sécurité des jeunes enfants. L'Australie a été le premier pays à interdire aux moins de 16 ans d'utiliser les réseaux sociaux en décembre. Maintenant, la France envisage une loi similaire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 21 juillet, les députés français devraient voter une loi qui empêcherait les enfants de moins de 15 ans d'accéder à des plateformes comme Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok et YouTube. Cette loi vise à protéger les jeunes enfants contre les contenus nuisibles et les risques en ligne.

**Impact** – Si la loi est adoptée, la France rejoindra l'Australie en fixant un âge légal pour l'utilisation des réseaux sociaux. C'est important car cela montre un effort pour mieux protéger les enfants contre le cyberharcèlement, l'addiction et les contenus inappropriés.

**Prochaine étape** – Après le vote, si la loi est validée, les entreprises de réseaux sociaux devront vérifier l'âge des utilisateurs pour bloquer ceux de moins de 15 ans en France. D'autres pays pourraient observer et envisager des règles similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La France avance vers une loi interdisant les réseaux sociaux aux moins de 15 ans pour améliorer leur sécurité et leur bien-être en ligne.
articles.personality_title_es: Francia planea prohibir las redes sociales para menores de 15 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países están preocupados por cómo las redes sociales afectan la salud y seguridad de los niños pequeños. Australia fue el primer país en prohibir el uso de redes sociales a menores de 16 años en diciembre. Ahora, Francia está considerando una ley similar.

**Qué pasó** – El 21 de julio, los legisladores franceses esperan votar una ley que prohibiría a los niños menores de 15 años usar plataformas como Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok y YouTube. Esta ley busca proteger a los niños de contenido dañino y riesgos en línea.

**Impacto** – Si la ley se aprueba, Francia se unirá a Australia estableciendo una edad mínima legal para usar redes sociales. Esto es importante porque muestra un esfuerzo por mantener a los niños más seguros y enfrentar problemas como el ciberacoso, la adicción y la exposición a contenido inapropiado.

**Próximo paso** – Después de la votación, si se aprueba la ley, las compañías de redes sociales deberán verificar la edad para bloquear a usuarios menores de 15 años en Francia. Otros países podrían observar y considerar reglas similares.

**Resumen en una frase** – Francia avanza con una ley para prohibir el uso de redes sociales a menores de 15 años y mejorar su seguridad y bienestar en línea.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle guardian figure made of soft, flowing light gently closing a glowing gate shaped like a smartphone screen, with playful, colorful silhouettes of children safely gathered nearby under a protective, arching ribbon of blue and red hues symbolizing France’s flag, all rendered in natural, muted tones.
